column1: # Per CLAUDE.md "Argument Ordering": at the call site a word may addq
# /s to force all args from the stack (forwardLimit=0) or /f to force
# all args from forward (forwardLimit=N). They override the sig's
# declared BarrierPos. /f additionally forbids stack supplementation:
# if the forward scan can't fill every slot, the sig is rejected.
#   addq  — forwardPrecedence:true,  sig [Number Number]. Default barrier = 2.
# `dup` is the canonical stack-only witness now that neg/negq have
# been consolidated into a single forward-eligible `negq`. `dup`'s
# return is two copies of args[0], so the row outputs make the
#   addq  — [Number Number] forward-prec — args[1] + args[0] (decimal contagion)
# /s on a forward-precedence word: forward window closed, args must come from prefix.

column3: no stack values, /s forbids forward fill
one stack value, /s still needs sig[1] from stack

column1: # /f on a stack-only word: forward window opened to N.

column3: forces forward dispatch on default-stack-only word; arg comes from forward, dup emits twice
/f forbids stack fall-back; sig[0] must come from forward

column1: # /s on a stack-only word: redundant, identical to default.

column3: default stack-only with /s and no prefix

column1: # /f on a forward-precedence word: also redundant since the default barrier
# already lets forward fill every slot. The interesting bit is that /f
# disables stack supplementation, so a partial-forward call now fails.

column3: default would fall back to stack; /f forbids it
no forward args at all; /f rejects
